fed_monetary: The FOMC holds the federal funds rate at 3.50–3.75% for a second consecutive meeting, with St. Louis Fed President Musalem stating April 1 that 'policy is well positioned' and 'the current setting of the policy rate will remain appropriate for some time.' CME FedWatch assigns an 83% probability of no change at the May 6–7 meeting, with markets pricing at most one 25bp cut—most likely in September—as core PCE at 3.06% and PPI at 3.22% (vs. 2.6% est.) keep the bar for easing elevated. The Strait of Hormuz closure and attendant oil shock compound the Fed's stagflationary bind, forcing it to tolerate financial conditions tighter than it would prefer: the 10Y yield sits at 4.35%, VIX at 24.17, and the NFCI composite liquidity score at just the 35th percentile.
labor_data: March nonfarm payrolls surged +178K—nearly triple the +59K–100K consensus—reversing February's -133K strike-driven contraction, with job gains concentrated in healthcare; unemployment held at 4.3%, though the decline was driven partly by labor force withdrawal. Average hourly earnings rose less than expected, providing a sliver of dovish comfort on the wage-push inflation front, but the BLS notes the average workweek edged down to 34.2 hours, suggesting demand for labor hours is softening even as headline hiring recovers. Goldman Sachs now projects unemployment rising to 4.6% by year-end as the energy shock slows hiring, introducing a tail risk that could pressure the Fed toward a defensive cut.
fiscal_supply: A GAO report released April 3 warns that the deteriorating fiscal outlook poses systemic risks to Treasury's debt management capacity, with CBO projecting FY2026 deficits of $1.9 trillion and average annual deficits of $2.4 trillion through 2036; publicly held debt already exceeds $31 trillion as of February. Treasury must issue substantially more debt annually to finance operations and refinance maturing obligations, with TBAC primary dealers flagging 'significant uncertainty' in deficit estimates given unresolved tariff revenue flows and Iran-war supplemental spending. The TGA balance stands at $847.7B while RRP is effectively drained at $0.2B, limiting the Fed's ability to absorb supply shocks without reserve compression—bank reserves at $3,026.7B provide a buffer but the global liquidity composite at the 35th percentile flags underlying tightness.
corporate: High-yield OAS at 313bp and IG OAS at 109bp reflect moderate risk appetite, though both metrics face upside pressure as WTI crude at $114.74—up 3.1% on the session and 40%+ above the $80 equilibrium threshold—threatens corporate margins across energy-intensive sectors. Procter & Gamble CFO Andre Schulten has flagged tariffs as 'a 5-point headwind to core EPS growth in fiscal 2026,' and with the Iran conflict now adding an acute energy-cost shock, consensus earnings revisions are skewing negative. The VIX at 24.17 signals that implied volatility has not yet reached panic levels, but the combination of sticky inflation, geopolitical supply disruption, and hawkish Fed optionality leaves credit vulnerable to a spread-widening episode if the Strait of Hormuz remains closed beyond mid-April.
next_catalyst: The March CPI print—scheduled for release Friday, April 10 at 8:30 a.m. ET—is the dominant near-term catalyst, with consensus (FactSet) projecting +0.8% MoM and +3.1% YoY headline on the back of the Iran-driven energy spike; a print at or above that level would extinguish any residual probability of a May cut and could force a re-pricing of the terminal rate higher.
sources: Fed Official Says Monetary Policy Should Hold for Some Time — The MortgagePoint / St. Louis Fed (Apr 1, 2026)
